description: Address 5 security findings from a comprehensive platform security audit: unauthenticated secret exposure, unbounded memory growth, ReDoS vulnerability, sensitive header leakage, and insufficient exec file validation.

userStories.title: Tighten execFile validation to enforce __exec- prefix
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the browser extension's executeScript handler to only accept files with the __exec- prefix so that a compromised or malicious server cannot inject arbitrary adapter files via this code path.
acceptanceCriteria: The regex in handleBrowserExecuteScript validates execFile matches /^__exec-[a-f0-9-]+\.js$/
A request with execFile='slack.js' is rejected with -32602 error
Existing unit test in known-methods.test.ts still passes
userStories.notes: File: platform/browser-extension/src/browser-commands.ts line 933. Current regex: /^[a-z0-9_-]+\.js$/. Change to /^__exec-[a-f0-9-]+\.js$/ which matches the UUID-based filenames generated by writeExecFile() in platform/mcp-server/src/extension-protocol.ts (line 685: `${EXEC_FILE_PREFIX}${execId}.js` where execId is crypto.randomUUID()). This is a single-line regex change. No other callers use this code path for non-exec files.

userStories.title: Add ring-buffer cap to console log capture
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want console log entries per tab to be bounded by a configurable maximum so that high-volume console output cannot exhaust the service worker's memory.
acceptanceCriteria: CaptureState interface has a maxConsoleLogs field (number)
enableCapture() initializes maxConsoleLogs to 500 (matching the default for maxRequests)
When consoleLogs.length >= maxConsoleLogs, the oldest entry is evicted before pushing the new one (shift + push, same pattern as requests ring buffer on line 145-146)
The browser_enable_network_capture tool definition accepts an optional maxConsoleLogs parameter
userStories.notes: File: platform/browser-extension/src/network-capture.ts. The requests array already uses a ring-buffer pattern (lines 144-156). Apply the same pattern to consoleLogs at line 200. Add maxConsoleLogs to CaptureState (line 27-35), set it in enableCapture(), and check it before pushing in the Runtime.consoleAPICalled handler. Also update the browser_enable_network_capture tool in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/enable-network-capture.ts to accept and forward the optional maxConsoleLogs param (follow the same pattern as maxRequests). Console logs don't need the requestIdToIndex map since they have no secondary lookups.

userStories.title: Add wildcard count limit to URL pattern validation to prevent ReDoS
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want URL pattern validation to reject patterns with excessive path wildcards so that the glob-to-regex conversion in matchPattern cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking.
acceptanceCriteria: validateUrlPattern() in shared/src/index.ts rejects patterns with more than 5 asterisks in the path portion
The error message clearly states the limit: 'URL pattern has too many wildcards in path (max 5)'
Existing unit tests for validateUrlPattern still pass
userStories.notes: File: platform/shared/src/index.ts, validateUrlPattern() at line 293. After the existing match regex extracts the path (match[3]), count the number of '*' characters in it. If > 5, return an error message. This is a 3-line addition after line 303. The limit of 5 is generous — real Chrome match patterns rarely use more than 1-2 wildcards in the path. The shared package needs rebuilding (bun run build covers this via tsc --build project references). Test file: platform/shared/src/validation.test.ts — add test cases for wildcard limit.

userStories.title: Scrub sensitive headers from network capture before returning to MCP clients
userStories.description: As a user, I want my authentication tokens and session cookies to be automatically redacted from captured network requests so that they do not leak into AI conversation context.
acceptanceCriteria: A SENSITIVE_HEADERS set is defined containing: authorization, cookie, set-cookie, x-csrf-token, x-xsrf-token, proxy-authorization (all lowercase for case-insensitive comparison)
getRequests() scrubs requestHeaders and responseHeaders by replacing sensitive header values with '[REDACTED]'
The header key is preserved (so the AI can see that an Authorization header exists) but the value is replaced
Header comparison is case-insensitive (HTTP headers are case-insensitive per RFC 7230)
userStories.notes: File: platform/browser-extension/src/network-capture.ts, getRequests() function (around line 220). Add a scrubHeaders helper that takes a Record<string, string> | undefined and returns a new object with sensitive values replaced by '[REDACTED]'. Apply it to both requestHeaders and responseHeaders in the returned CapturedRequest objects. The SENSITIVE_HEADERS set should use lowercase keys and the comparison should lowercase the header name. Do NOT modify the stored data — only scrub at the getRequests() return boundary so internal state remains intact for potential future use. Pattern: `const scrubHeaders = (headers?: Record<string, string>): Record<string, string> | undefined => { if (!headers) return undefined; const scrubbed: Record<string, string> = {}; for (const [k, v] of Object.entries(headers)) { scrubbed[k] = SENSITIVE_HEADERS.has(k.toLowerCase()) ? '[REDACTED]' : v; } return scrubbed; };`

userStories.title: Require authentication on /ws-info endpoint
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the /ws-info endpoint to require Bearer authentication when a secret is configured so that local processes cannot steal the WebSocket secret by fetching this unauthenticated endpoint.
acceptanceCriteria: GET /ws-info applies checkBearerAuth(req, state.wsSecret) before returning the response
When wsSecret is configured: requests without a valid Bearer token receive 401 Unauthorized
When wsSecret is null (no secret configured): requests are allowed through unchanged
The Chrome extension's ws-info fetch includes the Bearer token from its stored config
Existing E2E tests still pass (test fixtures may need the auth header added)
userStories.notes: File: platform/mcp-server/src/http-routes.ts lines 147-153. Add `const authError = checkBearerAuth(req, state.wsSecret); if (authError) return authError;` before the response — same pattern as /reload and /extension/reload on lines 187-188 and 208-209. IMPORTANT: The Chrome extension fetches /ws-info from the offscreen document (platform/browser-extension/src/offscreen/index.ts) to get the WebSocket URL and secret. Currently it does a plain GET. After this change, the extension needs to include the secret as a Bearer token. The bootstrapping problem: the extension needs the secret to call /ws-info, but /ws-info is how it gets the secret. The solution is that when a secret is configured, the extension must already know it (via chrome.storage.local config set by the side panel or MCP server handshake). Check the offscreen document's fetchWsUrl() function to understand the current flow and update it. If the extension currently has no way to know the secret before calling /ws-info, an alternative approach is to rate-limit /ws-info aggressively (max 3 calls per minute) and only return the secret to requests from chrome-extension:// origins. The Origin header check already exists on line 109-111.
